title: The Dwelling Built Into Ancient Roots

features.name: the root-woven walls
features.desc-on-touch: The shed was not built here. It grew here. Roots have woven themselves into the frame, and wood into roots. You cannot tell where the structure ends and the earth begins.

features.name: the stored tools that rust into thought
features.desc-on-touch: Old implements hang here, their metal slowly becoming part of the soil. Each tool has left an impression deeper than its shape—a memory in the air.

features.name: the breathing hole
features.desc-on-touch: A gap in the root-wall where air moves in and out. The shed breathes with the earth. The rhythm is so slow you only notice it if you sit still for hours.

features.name: the floor that is no longer floor
features.desc-on-touch: Below your feet the boundary has dissolved. Soil and wood and root have become one substance. To walk here is to walk on something that is still deciding what it wants to be.

features.name: the root-archive shelf
features.desc-on-touch: Small objects sit here—photographs, letters, seeds in glass. Some have been here longer than the shelf itself. The roots are learning to keep them.

features.name: the door that opens downward
features.desc-on-touch: What the locals call a door actually opens into the deeper root-system. Descending here leads only further into earth. The dwelling extends indefinitely downward.

inscription: a dwelling older than the forest remembers building it; the roots have made their home inside it, and inside them, and the boundary keeps dissolving deeper

reverie.title: REVERIE — inside the ancient shed, listening to the roots br
narr: Inside the shed, she sits on something that might be a bench or
might be an old root worn smooth by hands. Rain drums on the roof
that is also the canopy. She doesn't speak. You watch water find
its way through the wood-root boundary and still keep going down.
'This place is older than us,' she finally says, 'and it's still
deciding whether we're part of it or just passing through.' You believe he

file.name: WesternRoots_TimelapsePhotos_1973-2019.txt
file.note: A set of photographs taken from the same angle over 46 years. The shed slowly becomes the forest. The boundaries blur one photograph at a time.

exits.label: back to where pressure first learned to think
deeper into the root-paths where dwellings are still growing
down through the door that only opens into the earth
